items.question: What is Awdah?
items.answer: Awdah is a calm companion for people returning to missed prayers and fasts. It combines guided onboarding, clear progress tracking, and privacy-aware defaults so the experience stays steady and manageable.

items.question: How are Hijri dates calculated?
items.answer: We use the Umm al-Qura calendar system, the official civil Hijri calendar of Saudi Arabia and the most widely used standardised Hijri calendar globally.

items.question: Why not assume every month is 30 days?
items.answer: Assuming 30 days per month leads to significant cumulative errors. A real Hijri year is approximately 354–355 days, not 360. Using the Umm al-Qura calendar ensures months are correctly 29 or 30 days, Ramadan is identified accurately, and your qadaa is counted precisely.

items.question: What is the age of Bulugh in the app?
items.answer: If you do not know your exact date of Bulugh, the app offers a default helper that places it at 15 Hijri years from your date of birth. This follows the majority position used for onboarding guidance, and we only apply it when you explicitly choose the default.

items.question: How is 'missed days' calculated?
items.answer: Qadaa only covers the 'gaps' in your practicing history. Start point: your date of Bulugh. End point: the current date. Exclusions: any practicing periods you add. Formula: (Total Days from Bulugh to Today) − (Total Days in Practicing Periods).

items.question: Which prayers are included in qadaa?
items.answer: The five daily obligatory (Fard) prayers: Fajr, Dhuhr, Asr, Maghrib, and Isha.

items.question: What about Witr?
items.answer: In v1, Witr is not tracked as a separate qadaa obligation. The app focuses on the five agreed-upon fard prayers, while noting that the Hanafi school treats Witr differently from the majority view.

items.question: Is there a time limit on making up missed prayers?
items.answer: No. The obligation remains until it is fulfilled. The app follows the majority view that missed prayers should be made up, while acknowledging that some scholars place stronger emphasis on repentance for deliberate abandonment. Making up even one prayer per day is valid and valued.

references.label: IslamWeb: missed prayers in Hanbali fiqh

items.question: How does the app calculate missed fasts?
items.answer: The app identifies every Ramadan that occurred during your gap periods and counts the actual days of that Ramadan (29 or 30) using the Umm al-Qura calendar, with no hardcoded values.

items.question: What about fasts broken intentionally (Kaffarah)?
items.answer: Kaffarah is out of scope in v1. The app tracks the qadaa side only. If a fast was broken intentionally without a valid excuse, you may also owe kaffarah, so please confirm your case with a qualified scholar.

references.label: Islamic Relief: missed fasts and kaffarah

items.question: What about Ramadan exemptions for women?
items.answer: Menstruation exemptions for Sawm are not modeled in v1. That means the current count may be higher than the actual obligation for some women until this is supported in a future version.

references.label: Islamic Relief: missed fasts and menstruation

items.question: Is my religious data sensitive?
items.answer: Yes. Religious practice data is sensitive personal data. Stored app data is encrypted at rest in managed cloud infrastructure. Your logs remain private to you, we do not sell or share them, and authentication is handled by the cloud sign-in service so raw passwords are never stored by the app.

items.question: Why don't practicing periods automatically fill in my prayer logs?
items.answer: When you add a practicing period, Awdah subtracts those days from your qadaa debt mathematically, so no rows are created. Auto-generating thousands of entries would embed a false timestamp on every row, all showing the moment onboarding was saved rather than when each prayer was actually made. It would also invert the mental model for any prayer you missed inside an otherwise practicing period and create large batches of database records that are slower to query or delete. The debt number is correct without the rows.

items.question: How is the revert (convert) date handled in the calculation?
items.answer: For a new Muslim, obligations begin at the moment of shahada, not at puberty. If your revert date is after your bulugh date, Awdah uses your revert date as the start of your qadaa calculation. If your revert date is at or before bulugh (for example, you converted as a child), bulugh remains the start date. The effective start is always: max(bulugh date, revert date).

items.question: Is the 'after change' debt estimate shown before saving accurate?
items.answer: It is a frontend estimate based on your current practicing periods and the proposed new dates. It is calculated using the same formula as the authoritative backend calculation. The backend will recompute the exact figure after you confirm and save, and the dashboard will refresh with the precise number.

items.question: Why does the app use UTC for all date calculations?
items.answer: Using UTC avoids Daylight Saving Time drift. A prayer logged at midnight local time could shift to the previous or next Hijri day if the app used a local timezone. UTC keeps religious dates stable regardless of where you travel or whether DST applies in your region. Gregorian dates shown in the UI are converted to your local timezone for display only.

items.question: How is the Momentum score calculated?
items.answer: Momentum is strictly tied to your daily obligations. The streak walks backwards from today and counts every consecutive Hijri day on which you completed all 5 obligatory prayers. It represents your current 'Momentum' in maintaining your primary duty. If you miss even one obligatory prayer today or yesterday, the Momentum streak resets.

items.question: How do individual prayer and fasting streaks work?
items.answer: Unlike the main Momentum streak, individual streaks (like your 'Fajr streak' or 'Fasting streak') are inclusive. They count any day you performed that act, whether as a daily obligation, a make-up (qadaa), or during Ramadan. This 'Persistence' model ensures that your effort in recovering missed prayers is always recorded and celebrated, even if your main Momentum is interrupted.
